coredata.dc:title: Nonlinear optical responses of carbon quantum dots anchored on graphene oxide hybrid in solid-state transparent monolithic silica gel glasses
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Chan
Huang, Li
Guo, Qiaohang
Chen, Wenzhe
Li, Wei
coredata.dc:description:
Exploring the nonlinear optical (NLO) response of graphene-based hybrids in solid-state three-dimensional macrostructures is vital to promote the practical development of graphene-based photonic and optoelectronic devices. Here, a solid-state hybrid of ultrafine and well-dispersed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported on graphene oxide (GO) was chosen as representative of such materials. The hybrid was introduced into organically modified silica gel glasses using a facile sol-gel wet chemical technique to obtain stable transparent monolithic nanocomposites, and their practical application in nonlinear optics was explored. The NLO performances were investigated by the open-aperture (OA) and closed-aperture (CA) Z-scan techniques in the nanosecond regime using a laser with a wavelength of 532 nm. The as-prepared CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses displayed completely different NLO behavior from that of a CQD/GO suspension. The NLO performance of CQD/GO significantly improved after incorporation into SiO2 gel glasses because of the more graphitized structure of the CQD/GO hybrid. Both the OA and CA Z-scan patterns suggested that the NLO properties of the CQD/GO suspension combined nonlinear absorption and nonlinear scattering, while the NLO response of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses stemmed primarily from nonlinear absorption and nonlinear refraction. In addition, this is the first report of the third-order nonlinear susceptibility
χ
( 3 )
of a CQD/GO hybrid doped in solid-state gel glasses. The present results therefore demonstrate the feasibility and versatility of CQD/GO silica gel glasses, a promising new class of highly efficient NLO materials that can be applied in optical communications, radiofrequency optoelectronics, and all-optical signal processing.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sol-gel process
Hybrid
Nanocomposites
Graphene
Carbon quantum dots
Nonlinear optical response

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the CQD/GO hybrid nanostructures
Synthesis of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–d): TEM and HRTEM images of CQD/GO hybrid; (e) and (f): SEM images of silica gel glasses and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses. Insert in (e) and (f): photographs of the SiO2 gel glass and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses.
FT-IR spectra of (a) CQD/GO and (b) CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses.
Raman spectra of (a) CQD/GO and (b) CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses.
TGA curves of SiO2 matrix and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses.
(a) UV–vis spectra and (b) diagram used to calculate the bandgap of CQD/GO and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses.
PL spectra (λex = 320 nm) of (a) CQD and CQD SiO2 gel glasses and (b) CQD/GO and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses at room temperature.

OA Z-scan curves of (a) SiO2 matrix, and CQD/GO suspension and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses with linear transmittance of (b) 87, (c) 78, and (d) 70% at an input fluence at focus of 1.14 GW/cm2. In (b), (c) and (d), black and red dots represent CQD/GO suspension and QD/GO SiO2 gel glasses, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
OA Z-scan curves of (a) CQD/GO suspension and (b) CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses at an input fluence at focus of 0.81 GW/cm2. Das: contribution of NLA and NLS; Da: contribution of NLA.

Representative (a) OA and (b) CA Z-scan curves of CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses with linear transmittance of 78% at an input fluence at focus of 1.43 GW/cm2. The black scattered squares indicate experimental data and the red solid line shows the best-fitted curve. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
One-step synthetic pathway to obtain CQD/GO multifunctional nanoarchitectures and composite silica gel glasses as optical limiters.
Nonlinear optical parameters of the fabricated CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses with different linear transmittances.

head.ce:title: Nonlinear optical responses of carbon quantum dots anchored on graphene oxide hybrid in solid-state transparent monolithic silica gel glasses

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, 3 Xueyuan Road, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Processing and Application, 3 Xueyuan Road, Fuzhou 350108, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Advanced Materials Processing and Application

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Fujian University of Technology, 3 Xueyuan Road, Fuzhou 350108, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Exploring the nonlinear optical (NLO) response of graphene-based hybrids in solid-state three-dimensional macrostructures is vital to promote the practical development of graphene-based photonic and optoelectronic devices. Here, a solid-state hybrid of ultrafine and well-dispersed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported on graphene oxide (GO) was chosen as representative of such materials. The hybrid was introduced into organically modified silica gel glasses using a facile sol-gel wet chemical technique to obtain stable transparent monolithic nanocomposites, and their practical application in nonlinear optics was explored. The NLO performances were investigated by the open-aperture (OA) and closed-aperture (CA) Z-scan techniques in the nanosecond regime using a laser with a wavelength of 532 nm. The as-prepared CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses displayed completely different NLO behavior from that of a CQD/GO suspension. The NLO performance of CQD/GO significantly improved after incorporation into SiO2 gel glasses because of the more graphitized structure of the CQD/GO hybrid. Both the OA and CA Z-scan patterns suggested that the NLO properties of the CQD/GO suspension combined nonlinear absorption and nonlinear scattering, while the NLO response of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses stemmed primarily from nonlinear absorption and nonlinear refraction. In addition, this is the first report of the third-order nonlinear susceptibility
χ
( 3 )
of a CQD/GO hybrid doped in solid-state gel glasses. The present results therefore demonstrate the feasibility and versatility of CQD/GO silica gel glasses, a promising new class of highly efficient NLO materials that can be applied in optical communications, radiofrequency optoelectronics, and all-optical signal processing.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid development of laser technology has greatly promoted its application in fields including scientific research, production and processing, medicine, and the military. However, high-intensity laser beams have the potential to harm the eyes, bare skin, photoelectric detection sensors, and sophisticated optical instruments. Therefore, the design and manufacture of laser protection devices has become a central theme of laser technology. Over the last few decades, many efforts have focused on the search for so-called optical limiting (OL) materials, which exhibit “nonlinear extinction”; i.e., they strongly attenuate intense, potentially dangerous laser beams, while readily transmitting low-intensity ambient light. The rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology is opening up new opportunities in the synthesis of OL materials, as well as the research and design of practical optical limiters. A variety of materials, ranging from organic dyes (such as phthalocyanine, porphyrin, and fullerene) [1–3], carbon nanomaterials (including carbon black, carbon nanotubes, and graphene) [4–6] to noble metal nanoparticles [7,8] and quantum dots [9,10], have been extensively investigated for their OL effects. Despite significant progress in the past decade in the development of OL nanomaterials and their nonlinear interaction with light, there is still an urgent need for highly OL-responsive components. In particular, integrating multiple functionalities into individual nanoscale hybrids with OL response to ultrafast laser pulses is of tremendous importance [11–15]. Compared with the individual constituents, hybrid nanomaterials often exhibit enhanced nonlinear optical (NLO) and OL effects and/or flexible processability, resulting from the unique structures and synergistic coupling effects between the constituents.

Two-dimensional materials provide a platform for strong light–matter interactions, creating wide-ranging design opportunities via new-material discoveries and new methods for geometrical structuring. Among these, graphene, a nanometer-thick two-dimensional analog of fullerenes and carbon nanotubes, has become one of the most hotly studied of all two-dimensional materials, including widespread research into its optoelectronic applications [16–18]. Furthermore, because its linear band structure allows interbands at all photon energies, graphene has been suggested as a material that might have large third-order
χ
( 3 )
nonlinearities. It has been confirmed that graphene nanosheets and their derivatives have excellent third-order NLO and OL performance over a broad wavelength range from the visible to the near infrared region, with versatile optical mechanisms such as saturable absorption (SA), two-photon absorption (TPA) and nonlinear scattering (NLS). These properties give rise to applications in nanophotonic devices, such as mode-lockers, optical limiters, and optical switches [19–23]. Furthermore, graphene and its derivatives, including graphene oxide (GO) and reduced GO (rGO), present two-dimensional structures and readily functionalizable chemistry, allowing them to be combined with complementary NLO materials in tandem configurations, or to be hybridized with other OL components. Therefore, they provide ideal platforms and matrices for constructing graphene hybrids [24–28].

For example, a series of nanoparticle (NP) composites of GO/noble metal (Au, Pt, and Pd) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal reaction, and the inclusion of metal NPs on the GO surfaces significantly enhanced the OL properties of GO through NLS effects [24]. Elsewhere, CuO–hydrogen-induced exfoliated graphene nanocomposites showed enhanced OL properties, which arose from the strong nonlinear absorption of semiconducting CuO nanoparticles in the femtosecond (fs) and nanosecond (ns) laser excitation regimes [25]. Both rGO–PbS and graphene–CdS nanohybrids exhibited better OL properties when compared with their naked counterparts upon both 532 and 1064 nm excitation in the ns regime, because of the charge transfer between the two components [26]. The above results strongly suggest that construction of nanohybrids is a facile and effective strategy to improve and tailor the NLO and OL responses of graphene, which consist of a combination of multiple nonlinear mechanisms, including reverse saturable absorption (RSA), TPA, and NLS, as well as photo-induced electron transfer.

Up to now, most studies of the NLO and OL properties of graphene-based nanocomposites have been conducted in solvents. Although liquid matrices allow composite materials to recover quickly from laser irradiation and provide considerable convenience for the study of their properties and mechanisms, they are not suitable for practical applications and device integration. Besides, suspensions of these materials inevitably suffer from agglomeration when deposited for a long period, resulting in deterioration of their NLO and OL properties. To address these problems, the incorporation of graphene-based composites into solid-state matrices could be an effective strategy. Therefore, an important current challenge is to develop methods for stably embedding graphene-based hybrids in an appropriate matrix, or assembling them into solid-state three-dimensional macrostructures, while retaining the inherent properties of the individual OL components. One promising class of solid-state matrices consists of sol–gel-derived, organically modified silicate gel glasses. With good optical transparency, high thermal stability, favorable mechanical characteristics, facile preparation technique, molecular-level uniformity of dopants, and designable microstructures, they represent highly suitable host materials for practical applications, especially in photonics and optoelectronics. Indeed, several research groups have already incorporated graphene into silica gel glasses, and demonstrated the maintenance of the inherent OL effects of graphene [29,30]. However, there has so far been no report of the third-order susceptibility
χ
( 3 )
of graphene in solid-state gel glass matrices in the ns regime. Such a discovery would be highly significant to promote the practical and device-compatible development of graphene-based nonlinear photonics.

In the present work, a nanohybrid of ultrafine and well-dispersed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) supported on GO nanosheets was synthesized by a one-step electrochemical method. The nanohybrid was subsequently introduced into organically modified silica gel glasses using the sol–gel wet chemical technique to obtain stable transparent monolithic nanocomposites and explore their practical application in nonlinear optics (Scheme 1 ). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) imaging, Raman spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy were performed to investigate the morphology, structure, and linear optical properties of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses. The NLO properties of the CQD-GO in water and Ormosil gel glass were investigated by the ns open-aperture (OA) and closed-aperture (CA) Z-scan techniques at 532 nm. The results were compared between the water and gel glass phases, and possible mechanisms for the enhancement of NLO behavior of the CQD/GO in the SiO2 gel glass were explored. Meanwhile, the third-order susceptibility of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses was also calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CQD/GO hybrid nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: The CQD/GO nanocomposites were synthesized by a facile electrochemical method. Typically, a graphite rod (99.99%, Alfa Aesar Co. Ltd.) was inserted into ultrapure water as the anode (18.4 MΩ cm−1, 500 mL), which was placed parallel to another graphite rod as the counter-electrode with a separation of 5.0 cm. Static potentials of 30 V were applied to the two electrodes using a direct current (DC) power supply. After continuous stirring for 120 h, the CQD/GO hybrid was obtained, the anode graphite rod became corroded, and a solution appeared gradually in the reactor. The solution was centrifuged to remove the precipitated graphite oxide and graphite particles and filtered and dialyzed with a cellulose ester membrane bag [MD77 (8000–14000)]. The solution was dialyzed and finally concentrated to prepare it for characterization. CQDs were also prepared for comparison purpose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses
ce:section.ce:para: Optically transparent CQD/GO-doped silica gel glasses were fabricated by hydrolysis and polycondensation of tetraethyloxysilane (TEOS), 3-glycidoxy-propyltrimethoxysilane [CH2OCHCH2O(CH2)3 Si(OCH3)3, GPTMS], and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane [NH2(CH2)3Si(OC2H5)3, APTES]. The molar ratio of (TEOS + GPTMS + APTES):ethanol:distilled water in the precursor was 1:4:4, and the molar ratio of TEOS:GPTMS:APTES was 7:2:1. N,N′-Dimethylformamide (DMF) was introduced as a solvent and drying-control chemical additive at a volume ratio of 0.6 parts DMF per 1 part ethanol. Specifically, 8.0, 2.3, 1.2, 12.1, and 3.7 mL of TEOS, GPTMS, APTES, ethanol, and aqueous CQD/GO, respectively, were mixed under ultrasonication for 30 min. Different amount of the aqueous CQD/GO can result in CQD/GO with different linear transmittance. Subsequently, 7.3 mL of DMF was gradually added to the mixture, which was continuously ultrasonicated for 6 h. The mixture was divided into several parts with equal volumes, individually cast into polystyrene cells, sealed, and left to age and dry for several weeks. All of the gel glasses were approximately 1.3 mm in thickness and 4 cm in diameter. The samples could be characterized without further processing. CQDs SiO2 gel glasses were also prepared for comparison purpose.

The morphology of the CQD/GO hybrids was investigated by TEM (JEM-2010, accelerating voltage 200 kV). The samples were ultrasonicated in ethanol to ensure dispersion. A drop of the dispersed sample was left to dry on a commercial carbon-coated Cu TEM grid. The morphology of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses was examined with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE SEM: JEOL JSM-6700, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). For the SEM observations, Au was deposited onto a freshly fractured surface of the sample by sputtering. FT-IR spectra were measured on a ThermoNICOLET6700 spectrometer. The samples were prepared as KBr pellets. Raman spectra of the CQD/GO hybrid composites and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were obtained using a Raman spectrometer (Renishaw Invia) at ambient temperature with an excitation wavelength of 785 nm. UV–vis spectra were recorded using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600). The absorption behaviors of the CQD/GO suspensions and gel glasses were measured in quartz cells with a path length of 10 mm. Fluorescence spectroscopy was performed on a Horbia Jobin Yvon FM-4 fluorescence spectrometer.

The NLO and OL behaviors of the CQD/GO hybrids in suspension and gel glasses were investigated by the OA and CA Z-scan techniques [31]. The excitation light source was a Nd:YAG laser (Brio 640, Quantel, Les Ulis, France) with a repetition rate of 1 Hz. The laser pulses (period 4 ns, wavelength 532 nm) were split into two beams by a mirror. The pulse energies at the front and back of the samples were monitored using energy detectors (PE25, Ophir Optronics Solutions Ltd., Jerusalem, Israel). The beam waist at the focus was 14.5 μm, and thus the Rayleigh length was 1.24 mm. All of the measurements were performed at room temperature. The samples were dispersed in water and added to 1-mm-thick glass cuvettes. The cuvettes were mounted on a computer-controlled translation stage that shifted the samples along the z axis. An error of ±5% in the transmittance data was observed during the measurement of far field transmittance by the detector.

The successful anchoring of CQDs on two-dimensional monolayer GO nanosheets was confirmed by TEM images, as shown in Fig. 1 (a) and (b). The average diameter of the decorating CQDs was approximately 2–3 nm (Fig. 1(b)) and the density of CQDs decorated on the GO nanosheets was found to be very high. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of GO and CQDs are displayed in Fig. 1(c) and (d), respectively, wherein the lattice fringes of the GO and CQDs, with d spacings of 4.316 and 1.998 Å, can be indexed to the (0 0 4) and (4 1 1) planes of carbon. Y. Liu et al. previously reported that the CQDs synthesized through electrochemical approach used pure water as the electrolyte exhibited plenty of oxygen-containing groups on its surface and therefore resulted in hydrophilic surface characteristics [32]. They also demonstrated that the oxygen functional groups were formed through the electrochemical oxidation and the sp2-hybridized CC in aromatic ring in graphite would partially decompose into oxygen functional groups in the CQDs. In our present study, the CQDs and GO were simultaneously formed during the electrochemical oxidation of graphite and would result in oxygen-containing group presence in the surfaces, which facilitates in constructing of CQD/GO hybrid through covalent bond. Consequently, the CQDs can well-disperse on GO surface even after centrifuging and filtering.

To assess its performance in optical applications, the CQD/GO hybrid was introduced into organically modified silica gel glasses through the sol–gel wet chemical technique. The inset images in Fig. 1(e) and (f) show photographs of the SiO2 gel glass and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses, respectively. In contrast to the colorless and transparent SiO2 gel glass, the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were light black, demonstrating the incorporation of CQD/GO hybrids. Furthermore, no cracks or any visible sediments were observed with the naked eye, confirming the homogeneous dispersion of CQD/GO in the solid matrix. All these characteristics indicate that the composite gel glasses were well-suited for optical applications without further processing. Meanwhile, fracture surfaces of the SiO2 gel glass and the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses are presented in Fig. 1(e) and (f). The SiO2 gel glasses were composed of round nanoparticles or aggregates that were small and homogeneous, and the surfaces appeared to have a finer texture than that of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses. The granules of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were larger and tended to form two-dimensional flakes, with a coarser surface texture. These characteristics can be attributed to the presence of the two-dimensional CQD/GO hybrid in the precursor, which might have influenced the hydrolysis and polycondensation of the TEOS particles and caused silica to grow around these TEOS templates through inhomogeneous nucleation, thus forming similarly shaped flaky granules.

FT-IR spectroscopy is a powerful tool used for investigating surface characteristics of carbon based materials. Fig. 2 shows the FT-IR spectra of CQD/GO and its counterpart silica gel glasses. Six peaks located at 3420 (OH), 1680 (CO), 1385 (aromatic CC), 1142 (COC), 625 and 536 cm−1 (aromatic ArH) are observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CQD/GO, indicating the large amount of hydrophilic surface groups on the surface of CQD/GO, which contributed to the formation of CQD/GO hybrid and ensured their homogenous dispersion in the SiO2 gel glasses. For the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses, four fundamental absorption bands were observed at 1066, 795, 660, and 460 cm−1, originating from the anti-symmetric stretching vibration of SiOSi, symmetric stretching vibration of the SiOSi, ring vibrations of [SiO4] tetrahedral units, and asymmetrical bending vibration of SiOSi, respectively [33,34]. A weak band was observed at 970 cm−1, which corresponded to the vibration of SiOH groups formed as a by-product of incomplete condensation of the silanes. The result suggested that the silanes were completely hydrolyzed and polymerized to form a silica gel glass network during the sol–gel process. Obviously, the bands of CQD/GO also can be detected in composites gel glasses demonstrates the incorporation of the hybrid. While the observed weaken in characteristic band of oxygen group further confirmed that the CQD/GO participated the hydrolysis and polycondensation of SiO2 gel glasses.

To further explore the structure of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses, we also recorded the Raman spectra of the synthesized hybrids and their silica gel glasses, as shown in Fig. 2. The spectra of the CQD/GO and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses clearly contain two main peaks in the range of 1000–2000 cm−1, which are termed the G band and D band, respectively. The G band centered at 1580 cm−1 is assigned to the E2g phonon of carbon sp2 atoms [35]. The D band at 1350 cm−1 is the breathing mode of the k-point phonon with A1g symmetry [35]. This peak mainly originates from lattice defects, such as vacancies, edge crimping, and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms linked to the oxygen-containing groups. Generally, the intensity ratio of the D peak to the G peak (I D/I G) is used as an indicator of the level of functionalization. The I D/I G ratios for CQD/GO and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses are 1.09 and 0.81, respectively. The decrease in the ratio indicates that the structure of CQD/GO became more graphitized after incorporation into silica gel glasses. This suggests that the oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl, on the CQDs and GO might have participated in hydrolysis and polycondensation during the sol–gel procedure, resulting in a more ideally sp2-conjugated structure of the graphene component. To confirm the above assumption, the silica gel glass and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were investigated by TGA, and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 . Clearly, the weight loss of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses was less than that of the silica gel glass, as shown in Fig. 4 . This implies that the encapsulation of the CQD/GO hybrid in the silica gel glasses promoted the hydrolysis and polycondensation of the precursor, resulting in gel glasses with a lower content of water and organic solvent, and a more compact structure.

The linear optical properties of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were studied by UV–vis spectroscopy, with the CQD/GO hybrid suspension also investigated for comparison. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), aqueous suspensions of the CQD/GO featured two absorption peaks at 215 and 262 nm. The former broad characteristic absorption peak was ascribed to π–π* transitions of aromatic CC bonds, and the latter band was attributed to n–π* transitions of CO [36,37]. In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses, the presence of both the two above-mentioned absorption bands further confirmed the successful introduction of the CQD/GO hybrid nanostructure into the silica gel glass. Note that the bands at 215 and 262 nm were red-shifted in the gel glasses, which can be explained by the participation of the CQD/GO hybrid in the hydrolysis and polycondensation of the sol–gel, which might have influenced (i) the delocalized states on the carbon surface, and (ii) the spatial structure of the CQDs and GO and their mutual steric repulsion.

Meanwhile, as it is well known that graphene materials behave as direct-bandgap semiconductors, the optical bandgap of the CQD/GO suspension and gel glasses could be determined via the extrapolation of the following equation:
(1)
α = A
h ν -
E g
n / 2
/
h ν
where α, ν, Eg , A, and n are the absorption coefficient, incident light frequency, bandgap, a constant, and an integer, respectively. By plotting (αhν)2 versus hν, Eg was obtained via extrapolation of the absorption edge. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the Eg values of the CQD/GO suspension and gel glass were 2.88 and 3.35 eV, respectively. Clearly, the bandgap of CQD/GO become wider after incorporation into silica gel glass. This confirms that there were large amounts of oxygen-containing groups (such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl, and epoxy groups) present on the edges and planes in the CQD/GO hybrid, which provided active sites to form chemical bonds, allowing the surface of the CQD/GO hybrid to be modified. Such surface modification influence on the electronic property of GO had been theoretically and experimentally demonstrated [38,39]. After the introduction of CQD/GO into the silica gel glass, the presence of COOH and OH on the surface of CQD/GO enabled it to participate in the hydrolysis and polycondensation process. Hence, the surface of the CQD/GO could in principle be modified by introducing different chemical terminators at the edges, which would create a potential difference, resulting in a spin change in the band structure, thereby tuning the bandgap of the CQD/GO hybrid.

The linear optical of CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were further explored by PL spectroscopy and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . The results of CQDs and corresponding SiO2 gel glasses were also included for comparison purposes. Upon excitation at 320 nm, the CQDs exhibit one broad and strong emission band at around 430 nm (Fig. 6(a)). This is caused by both an intrinsic band gap, resulting from confined sp2 conjugation in the core of the CQDs, and extrinsic fluorescence, resulting from a surface state that can be excited either directly or by energy transfer from an intrinsic band. Compared to pristine CQDs, the characteristic emission of the CQD/GO was dramatically reduced (Fig. 6(b)), suggesting a PL quenching effect in the CQD/GO hybrid. Interestingly, although GO is itself fluorescent, it can also quench fluorescence [40]. The sp2 domains within GO allow quenching of nearby fluorescent species such as dyes, conjugated polymers, and quantum dots. Studies indicate that this quenching effect originates from fluorescence resonance energy transfer, or non-radiative dipole–dipole coupling, between the fluorescent species and GO [16]. In addition, strong PL quenching effect can be observed in both CQD and CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses. The surfaces of the CQDs and CQD/GO were changed on introduction into the silica matrix through the sol-gel procedure. This might cause the optical selection of different surface effects in the CQDs and CQD/GO. Furthermore, in the gel glass, the micro-environment of the CQDs and CQD/GO was completely different from that in aqueous solution. The pores existing in the gel glasses would likely produce strong scattering, also resulting in partial self-quenching of the CQDs and CQD/GO.

The NLO and OL responses of the CQD/GO hybrid in aqueous suspension and gel glasses were measured at 532 nm by the OA Z-scan technique, using laser pulses of 7 ns duration, and the results are displayed in Fig. 7 . The OA Z-scan curve of SiO2 matrix displayed in Fig. 7(a) suggests that on obvious NLO signal can be detected and the observed NLO mainly comes from doping CQD/GO hybrid. The depth of the valley in an OA Z-scan curve directly indicates the extent of NLO and OL behavior. Clearly, the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses present an even better OL effect than the solution-phase CQD/GO with the same linear transmittance (Fig. 7(b)–(d)), which suggests immediate applications in fabricating high-performance optical products and devices. The enhanced NLO and OL behaviors in the solid-state matrix might have arisen from the following two factors: (1) A different micro-environment surrounding the CQD/GO hybrid. CQD/GO particles are highly mobile in solution, allowing them to readily aggregate; in contrast, the gel glasses function as solid nano-containers to fix the CQD/GO particles and block the aggregation of individual CQDs, thus enhancing their NLO and OL properties. (2) The structure of CQD/GO becomes more graphitized after incorporation into silica gel glasses. As shown in Fig. 3, the I D/I G ratio in the Raman spectra of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses is considerably less than 1 and clearly lower than that of solution-phase CQD/GO. This suggests that the oxygen-containing groups participate in hydrolysis and polycondensation during the sol–gel procedure, resulting in a more ideally sp2-conjugated structure of the graphene component. This structure will transmit crystal lattice vibrations more rapidly, which consequently increases the thermal conductivity. As a result, the energy absorbed by the multi-component hybrids can be more efficiently transferred to the surrounding matrices, hence improving the NLO and OL responses.

Depending on the linear absorption spectrum of the sample, and the excitation wavelength, laser fluence, intensity, pulse width and repeated frequency, OL and NLO behaviors can originate from diverse processes such as nonlinear absorption (NLA) (including two-photon absorption, free-carrier absorption, and reverse saturable absorption), NLS, and nonlinear refraction (NLR). A symmetric peak, such as those exhibited in the OA Z-scan curves of CQD/GO suspension and gel glasses, generally implies the presence of NLA and/or NLS. To separate the contributions of NLA and NLS, we used a Z-scan setup following Jourdier and Rao [41,42] and the results are shown in Fig. 5. The coefficient α 2 is a sum of contributions from both NLA (α 2A) and NLS (α 2S): α 2 = α 2A + α 2S. The value for α2 is obtained by fitting the T(z) equation to the experimental data. In the context of our experiment, i.e., the Z-scan in Fig. 8 , curve (b) gives α 2, and curve (a) is fitted with α 2A. Thus, α 2S can be extracted. The values of α 2, α 2A, and α 2S were calculated to be 4.86, 4.48, and 0.38 for the CQD/GO suspension, and 5.69, 5.66, and 0.03 (× 10−9 cm/W) for the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses.

It is worth noting that the α 2S of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses is almost negligible in comparison with that of the suspension. This can be readily explained by the mechanism of NLS and the different environments of the liquid and solid-state matrices. NLS signals originate from the photo-induced excitation, ionization, and heating of nanomaterials, giving rise to their expansion and making them into scattering centers. Furthermore, energy transfer to the solvent results in the formation of microbubbles, which act as secondary centers and lead to a further reduction in transmittance with increasing input laser fluence. The latter is indeed the dominant contribution to the observed NLS herein. For the gel glasses, the rigid solid environment obviously precludes the formation of microbubbles and therefore results in the elimination of NLS.

By the CA Z-scan technique, we can conveniently and effectively distinguish between NLA and NLR. In the CA Z-scan of the CQD suspension, the sample failed to yield any appreciable signal, which confirmed that NLR made no contribution to the observed NLO and OL behavior of the CQD/GO in suspension. This is consistent with the previous finding that the NLR of graphene in a liquid matrix in the ns regime is mainly attributed to the transient thermal effect and is difficult to observe because of the much stronger non-transient thermal effect. In contrast, appreciable NLR can be discerned in the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses (Fig. 9 ), owing to the fact that when incorporated in silica gel glasses, the surrounding solid-state matrices exclude the contribution of NLS, making the NLR effect more easily observed. It can be seen from Fig. 9(b) that the n 2 value of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses was negative because of the self-defocusing effect. Moreover, both the OA and CA Z-scan patterns implied that the OL effects of the CQD/GO suspension combined NLA and NLS, while the OL response of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses stemmed primarily from NLA and NLR. The OA and CA Z-scan measurements allowed easy calculation of the nonlinear parameters for the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses. The corresponding values of the nonlinear parameters of three CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses were calculated using Z-scan theory [31] and are listed in Table 1 . Apparently, both β and n 2 of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses gradually increase with the decrease linear transmittance.

Remarkably, the experimental β value of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses is 10−9 cm/W, which is comparable to those recently reported in covalently functionalized graphene-oxide-incorporated silica gel glasses (β ≈ 10−9 cm/W) [29], graphene oxide nanosheets and graphene oxide nanoribbons in Ormosil gel glass (β ≈ 10−9 cm/W) [30], h-BN nanosheets–gel hybrid (β ≈ 10−9 cm/W) [43], and graphene/Ag2S organic glasses (β ≈ 10−9 cm/W) [44]. The results indicate that the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses can be considered as potential candidates for optical limiters.

In addition, the n 2 of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses is 10−13 cm2/W, which is similar to those of previously reported graphene–silicon hybrid optoelectronic devices using the four-wave mixing method with picosecond (ps) pulses (7.7 × 10−13 cm2/W) [45], and GO in DMF using the Z-scan technique with ns pulses (10−13 cm2/W) [46], while it is 6 orders of magnitude smaller than that in Zhang’s work [47], in which the nonlinear refractive index n 2 was calculated using the Z-scan method with ps pulses. The third-order susceptibility of graphene probably has multiple origins, such as electronic polarization, molecular reorientation effects, population redistribution (or excited-states refraction), free-carrier refraction, and thermal effects. In addition, the nonlinear refractive index of graphene is dependent on the input pulse energy, pulse width, and beam waist radius, which decrease with the increasing input pulse energy and beam waist radius but increase with increasing pulse width [46,47]. However, such materials also feature a strong nonparametric process, which gives rise to SA, and which presumably also gives rise to a strong nonparametric contribution to n 2. In the present work, the high input pulse energy excludes the presence of SA and hence eliminates the contribution of SA in the n 2. Interestingly, the calculated χ(3) of the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses is 1.39 × 10−11 esu, which is 4 times larger than that of graphene embedded in a polymer matrix of polyvinyl alcohol film (3.3 × 10−12 esu) [48]. Hendry et al. showed that graphene has a highly effective nonlinear susceptibility χ(3) (∼10−7 esu) using four-wave mixing [49] while Couris et al. reported the χ(3) of graphene fluoride in fluorosurfactant–water solutions to be 0.67 × 10−13 esu by Z-scan measurements [50]. The above results demonstrate that the CQD/GO SiO2 gel glasses are a very promising candidate for optical switching and optical limiter applications.

A solid-state hybrid of ultrafine and well-dispersed CQDs supported on GO was introduced into organically modified silica gel glasses using a facile sol–gel wet chemical technique to obtain stable transparent monolithic nanocomposites. The method can be readily extended to the fabrication of solid-state graphene-based nanocomposite optical materials, which would greatly promote the potential application of graphene in nanophotonics. The NLO performances were investigated by the OA and CA Z-scan techniques in the ns regime using a laser with a wavelength of 532 nm. The as-prepared CQD-GO/SiO2 gel glasses offer a significantly improved NLO response compared with CQD/GO suspension because of the more graphitized structure of the CQD/GO hybrid after incorporation into silica gel glasses and the combined mechanisms of NLA and NLR. We also showed that the third-order nonlinear susceptibility
χ
( 3 )
of the graphene-based hybrid in solid-state silica gel glasses was 1.39 × 10−11 esu. The present results therefore demonstrate the feasibility and versatility of CQD/GO silica gel glasses as a new and promising class of highly efficient NLO materials, which can be considered as potential candidates in optical communications, radiofrequency optoelectronics, and all-optical signal processing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61108056), Major Projects of the University of Fujian Province (Grant No. 2015N5007), and the Youth Natural Fund Key Project of Fujian Province (Grant No. JZ160462).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: J. Opt. Soc. Am. B: Opt. Phys.
